slides.title: Fold a box from a sheet of paper
slides.body: Take a real 8.5 × 11 sheet. Cut an equal square from each corner, fold the four sides up, and you get an open box. Different corner cuts give different boxes — set a cut, fold it, and watch the volume.

slides.title: Write the volume as a formula
slides.body: You felt the volume rise and fall as the cut changed. To pin down the best cut exactly, first capture the volume as a function of the cut x.

feedback.wrong: Not quite. After cutting x from each corner, the base is (8.5 − 2x) by (11 − 2x) and the height is x. Multiply V = x · (8.5 − 2x) · (11 − 2x) out.

slides.title: Find the best cut with calculus
slides.body: At the biggest box, the volume stops rising — its slope is zero. So differentiate V(x), find where the slope is zero, and read off the maximum volume.

feedback.wrong: Not yet. Differentiate each term of V(x) with the power rule.

slides.title: Now try a different sheet
slides.body: Here is a fresh 12 × 12 sheet. Use the same method — derivative, set it to zero, plug back in — to find the biggest box you can fold from it.

feedback.wrong: Differentiate V(x), set V′(x) = 0, and keep the root that fits a real box.
